bytes.topic: Routing — defining routes, route parameters, grouping and prefixing
bytes.title: Hono Routing — Parameters, Grouping, and app.route()
bytes.body:
Hono's router is one of the fastest available, powered by RegExpRouter and TrieRouter under the hood. Define routes directly on the `app` instance using `app.get()`, `app.post()`, etc. Route parameters are captured with `:param` syntax and accessed via `c.req.param('param')`.

For optional segments use `:param?`, for wildcards use `*`. To group related routes, instantiate a new `Hono` app and mount it onto the parent with `app.route('/prefix', subApp)`. This keeps handlers modular without touching the parent router's internals.

Path parameters in the sub-app are relative to its mount point — `/users/:id` on a sub-app mounted at `/api/v1` resolves to `/api/v1/users/:id`. You can also chain methods on the same path using `app.on(['GET', 'POST'], '/path', handler)` or the `.all()` shorthand. For type-safe params, pair routes with Zod validators via `zValidator('param', schema)` from `hono/validator`.

bytes.topic: Middleware — request pipeline, global vs route-specific, ordering matters
bytes.title: Hono Middleware — Pipeline Order, Global vs Route-Scoped
bytes.body:
Hono's middleware runs in the order it's registered, and that order is everything. Global middleware registered via `app.use('*', ...)` runs before every route handler. Route-scoped middleware passed directly into route definitions runs only for that path. The critical detail: middleware registered *after* a route definition won't intercept that route.

Hono uses a `Context` object (`c`) passed through the chain. Each middleware calls `await next()` to hand off to the next handler — skip it and the chain stops. This is how you build abort-early patterns like auth guards.

For route-specific middleware, pass it as an argument before the handler: `app.get('/admin', authMiddleware, handler)`. This keeps concerns local without polluting global scope. You can also scope to a sub-path with `app.use('/api/*', rateLimitMiddleware)` — only routes under `/api/` are affected.

A common gotcha: logging middleware registered after `app.route()` mounts misses those routes entirely. Always register cross-cutting concerns before mounting sub-routers.

bytes.topic: Dependency Injection — built-in DI containers vs manual wiring, testability impact
bytes.title: Hono Dependency Injection — Manual Wiring via Context vs External Containers
bytes.body:
Hono has no built-in DI container. Instead, it leans on its `Context` object as a lightweight service locator. You attach dependencies once at app startup using `app.use()` middleware, then retrieve them anywhere via `c.get()`. This pattern — sometimes called 'context injection' — is deliberately minimal: no decorators, no reflection, no IOC container overhead.

The tradeoff is testability. Because services live on `c`, unit-testing a handler means constructing a fake `Context` or using `app.request()` in integration style. For small apps this is fine. For larger codebases, teams wire Hono with external containers like `tsyringe` or `inversify`: the container resolves dependencies, handlers pull from `c.get()`, and the container itself is trivially swappable in tests.

The recommended pattern: define typed variables with `createFactory` (Hono v4+), bind services in a single middleware, and never import singletons directly inside handlers. This keeps handlers pure functions of context — easy to test, easy to swap.

bytes.topic: Project Structure — MVC vs vertical slice, feature folders, layering conventions
bytes.title: Hono Project Structure — Vertical Slices Beat MVC at Scale
bytes.body:
MVC feels natural at first in Hono — drop routes, controllers, and models into separate folders. But as your app grows, a feature change touches files across three directories. Vertical slices fix this by co-locating everything a feature needs.

In a vertical slice layout, each feature folder owns its Hono sub-app, validators, service logic, and types. You mount sub-apps onto the root app using `app.route()`. This means `/users` lives entirely in `src/features/users/` — its `router.ts` exports a `new Hono()` instance with all user-scoped routes, middleware, and handlers inline.

Layering still matters within each slice: keep pure business logic in a `service.ts`, HTTP concerns (parsing, response shaping) in the route handler, and Zod schemas in `schema.ts`. Hono's `zValidator` middleware slots cleanly into this — validate at the edge, pass typed data down to the service.

This structure scales horizontally: adding a `payments` feature means creating `src/features/payments/` and one `app.route('/payments', paymentsRouter)` call. No cross-feature coupling, no barrel-file sprawl.

bytes.topic: Input Validation — schema validation at the boundary, sanitization, error responses
bytes.title: Hono Zod Validator — Schema Validation at the Edge
bytes.body:
Hono's `@hono/zod-validator` middleware validates incoming data at the request boundary before your handler ever runs. Attach it per-route using `zValidator('json', schema)` — the first argument targets the target ('json', 'form', 'query', 'param', 'header'). On schema failure, Hono automatically returns a 400 with structured error details; you can override this by passing a custom hook as the third argument to control the error response shape.

Sanitization happens at the Zod layer: use `.trim()`, `.toLowerCase()`, and `.transform()` on fields so handlers receive clean data. Never trust `c.req.valid('json')` without first attaching the validator — it returns the raw body without coercion.

For nested routes or RPC mode, define schemas alongside route definitions so `hc` (the typed client) picks up the contract automatically. This gives you end-to-end type safety from schema definition through to the frontend fetch call, catching mismatches at compile time rather than runtime.

bytes.topic: Authentication — JWT validation, session handling, auth middleware patterns
bytes.title: Hono JWT Auth — Middleware, Validation, and Session Patterns
bytes.body:
Hono's `hono/jwt` middleware handles JWT validation with near-zero boilerplate. Mount `jwt({ secret })` on a route group and every request through it is automatically verified — no manual `verify()` calls needed. The decoded payload lands on `c.get('jwtPayload')`, giving you typed access to claims downstream.

For session-style auth, pair JWT with Hono's `setCookie`/`getCookie` helpers. Store a short-lived access token in an `HttpOnly` cookie and validate it with the same middleware. To scope auth to specific routes, use Hono's `app.use('/api/*', jwt({ secret }))` — unauthenticated requests get a 401 before your handler even runs.

For custom logic (role checks, token revocation via Redis), write a middleware that reads `c.get('jwtPayload')`, checks your store, then calls `next()` or returns early with `c.json({ error: 'Forbidden' }, 403)`. Chain it after `jwt()` on the same route group. Avoid re-verifying the token manually — trust the upstream middleware's result and focus your custom layer purely on authorization logic.

bytes.topic: Error Handling — global error handlers, consistent error response format
bytes.title: Hono Error Handling — Global Handlers and Consistent Error Responses
bytes.body:
Hono's `app.onError()` hook intercepts any unhandled error thrown inside route handlers or middleware, giving you a single place to shape every error response. Register it once after your routes — Hono calls it with the raw `Error` and the `Context`, so you can inspect `err.constructor` or a custom `statusCode` property before responding.

Pair this with a typed `HTTPException` (re-exported from `hono/http-exception`) so route handlers can throw structured errors without coupling to the response layer. `HTTPException` carries a `status` and optional `res` override, which `onError` can read directly via `err instanceof HTTPException`.

For a consistent envelope, always return `{ success, error: { code, message } }` — never let raw `err.message` leak into production. Use `c.json()` with the appropriate HTTP status so clients get both the status code and a machine-readable payload. Add a `notFound` handler via `app.notFound()` to cover unmatched routes with the same envelope, completing the contract.

bytes.topic: ORM & Database — query builders vs full ORMs, N+1 prevention, migrations
bytes.title: Hono + Drizzle — Killing N+1 Queries and Managing Migrations on the Edge
bytes.body:
Hono pairs naturally with Drizzle ORM — lightweight, no query-engine binary, and fully edge-compatible. Unlike Prisma, Drizzle works inside Cloudflare Workers and Deno Deploy where Hono commonly runs. Prisma's engine binary simply can't execute in those runtimes.

The N+1 problem surfaces fast in Hono route handlers. Fetching posts then looping to query comments per post fires N+1 round-trips to your D1 or Neon database. Drizzle's relational query API (`db.query.posts.findMany({ with: { comments: true } })`) collapses this into a single JOIN — no lazy-loading, no hidden waterfalls.

For query builders vs full ORMs: Drizzle sits in between. You get type-safe SQL composition (`.select().from().where()`) without surrendering control over the generated query. Prisma abstracts more but hides JOIN strategies behind magic — dangerous at scale.

For migrations, run `drizzle-kit generate` to diff your schema into SQL files, then `drizzle-kit migrate` to apply. Commit migration files alongside your Hono app. Never call `push` in production — it bypasses the migration history and can silently drop columns.

bytes.topic: Security — CORS, rate limiting, security headers, injection prevention
bytes.title: Hono Security Stack — CORS, Rate Limiting, and Hardened Headers in One Pass
bytes.body: Hono's middleware-first design lets you compose a full security layer before any route handler runs. Use `hono/cors` for CORS with strict origin allowlists — avoid `origin: '*'` in production. Pair it with `hono/secure-headers` which sets `X-Frame-Options`, `X-Content-Type-Options`, `Strict-Transport-Security`, and `Content-Security-Policy` in a single call. For rate limiting, `hono-rate-limiter` integrates natively with Hono's context and supports pluggable stores (memory, Redis via `@hono-rate-limiter/redis`). Against injection, Hono's `c.req.valid()` with Zod schemas is your first line — reject malformed input at the edge before it reaches business logic. Register middleware globally via `app.use('*', ...)` so no route is accidentally unprotected. Order matters: cors → secureHeaders → rateLimiter → your route-specific validators. Run security middleware before any auth middleware to drop bad requests cheaply. Never trust `c.req.header('X-Forwarded-For')` raw for rate-limit keys without validating the proxy chain first.
bytes.code_snippet:
import { Hono } from 'hono'
import { cors } from 'hono/cors'
import { secureHeaders } from 'hono/secure-headers'
import { rateLimiter } from 'hono-rate-limiter'

const app = new Hono()

app.use('*', cors({ origin: ['https://app.example.com'], credentials: true }))
app.use('*', secureHeaders())
app.use('*', rateLimiter({ windowMs: 60_000, limit: 100, keyGenerator: (c) => c.req.header('cf-connecting-ip') ?? 'unknown' }))

app.get('/api/data', (c) => c.json({ ok: true }))

export default app

bytes.topic: Performance — connection pooling, async handlers, response compression
bytes.title: Hono Performance — Connection Pooling, Async Handlers, and Response Compression
bytes.body:
Hono's edge-native design means performance pitfalls differ from Node.js frameworks. For connection pooling, avoid instantiating DB clients inside handlers — initialize once at the module level and inject via `app.use()` middleware using Hono's `c.set()`/`c.get()` context API. This prevents per-request connection churn, critical on Cloudflare Workers where cold starts are frequent.

Async handlers in Hono are natively awaited — no need for `asyncWrapper` shims. However, avoid `await`-ing inside loops; use `Promise.all()` to parallelize independent DB or API calls within a single handler.

For response compression, Hono's `compress()` middleware (from `hono/compress`) applies gzip/deflate negotiation automatically. On Workers runtime, compression is better delegated to the platform, but on Node.js (`@hono/node-server`) enabling it yields measurable wins for JSON-heavy endpoints. Pair with `c.header('Cache-Control', 'public, max-age=60')` on read-heavy routes to offload repeated computation entirely.

bytes.topic: Serialization — request/response DTOs, camelCase vs snake_case, null handling
bytes.title: Hono Serialization — DTOs, camelCase/snake_case, and Null Safety
bytes.body:
Hono's `c.json()` serializes whatever you pass it — no transformation layer by default. If your PostgreSQL columns are `snake_case` and your frontend expects `camelCase`, you need to map explicitly in your response DTOs rather than leaking raw DB rows.

Define typed response shapes and map at the handler boundary. For request bodies, use `c.req.json()` which returns `unknown` — pair it with Zod via `zod-validator` middleware to parse and strip unexpected fields before they reach business logic.

For null handling, Hono doesn't strip nulls on output. If a field is `null` and the client contract says it should be absent, filter it out in your DTO mapper. Alternatively, standardize on `null` everywhere and document it in your OpenAPI spec using `hono/zod-openapi`.

Avoid returning raw ORM objects from handlers — they often carry circular refs or internal fields that break `JSON.stringify` silently. Always project to a plain DTO. The `hono/zod-openapi` integration lets you co-locate schema, validation, and serialization shape in one place, making contract drift obvious at compile time.

bytes.topic: API Design — REST conventions, versioning strategy, OpenAPI generation
bytes.title: Hono API Design — Versioning and OpenAPI Generation Without the Boilerplate
bytes.body:
Hono's `@hono/zod-openapi` package collapses route definition, validation, and spec generation into a single source of truth. Swap `new Hono()` for `new OpenAPIHono()`, then define routes with `createRoute()` — passing Zod schemas for both input and output. The router infers types end-to-end and auto-generates a `/doc` OpenAPI 3.1 spec.

For versioning, use `app.route('/v1', v1Router)` and `app.route('/v2', v2Router)` — each an independent `OpenAPIHono` instance with its own `createRoute` definitions. This keeps v1 contracts frozen while v2 evolves. Mount a `/openapi.json` on each version prefix so consumers can target the exact spec they depend on.

Avoid path-level versioning inside a single router; it muddies the generated spec and couples version lifetimes. Prefer router-per-version — Hono's `route()` composition makes it zero-cost. Use `app.doc('/v2/openapi.json', { openapi: '3.1.0', info: { title: 'ByteAI API v2', version: '2.0.0' } })` to expose the scoped spec.

bytes.topic: Testing — unit vs integration, mocking HTTP, test database strategy
bytes.title: Hono Testing — Unit vs Integration with app.request()
bytes.body:
Hono's `app.request()` is the unit testing backbone — it invokes your route handler directly, returning a standard `Response` without spinning up a server. No `supertest`, no HTTP polyfill needed. To mock auth middleware, prepend `app.use()` in test setup to short-circuit JWT validation and inject a stub `userId` via `c.set()`.

Integration tests are different: use `serve()` from `@hono/node-server` (or Bun's native server), fire real `fetch()` requests, and tear down in `afterAll`. This validates middleware ordering, error serialization, and CORS headers end-to-end — things `app.request()` can miss.

For test databases, point `TEST_DATABASE_URL` at a dedicated schema. In `beforeAll`, run migrations via your ORM's push command or a raw `pg` client. In `afterEach`, truncate tables — never drop them. Dropping forces schema recreation on every test, which is slow and fragile.

The rule: unit-test handler logic and response shapes via `app.request()` with mocked middleware bindings; integration-test only the HTTP contract. Never duplicate both layers for the same route.

bytes.topic: Configuration — env vars, settings classes, secrets management
bytes.title: Hono — Type-Safe Env Vars and Secrets Management with env()
bytes.body:
Hono provides a built-in `env()` helper that abstracts environment access across runtimes — Cloudflare Workers bindings, Node.js `process.env`, and Bun's `Bun.env` all work through the same API without runtime-specific code.

In Cloudflare Workers, secrets and env vars are injected into the Worker's execution context, not `process.env`. Access them via `c.env` on the `Context` object — this is typed if you pass your bindings as a generic to `new Hono<{ Bindings: Env }>()`.

For Node.js or Bun, use `@hono/node-server` and read vars from `process.env` directly, or use the `env()` middleware from `hono/env` to inject a validated config object into the context. Pair this with Zod to validate and coerce types at startup — fail fast if `DATABASE_URL` is missing rather than crashing mid-request.

Never store secrets in `c.set()` across requests — context is per-request. For shared singletons like DB clients, initialize outside the `app` instance and close over them in route handlers.

bytes.topic: Logging — structured logs, correlation IDs, request logging middleware
bytes.title: Hono — Structured Logging with Correlation IDs and Request Middleware
bytes.body: Hono's `logger()` middleware logs basic request info, but production systems need structured JSON logs with correlation IDs to trace requests across services. Build this by composing a custom middleware before `logger()`. Use `c.set()` to store a generated request ID on the context, then attach it to every log entry downstream. Hono's `Context` object is your single source of truth — pull method, path, status, and duration from it after `await next()`. For correlation IDs from upstream services (e.g. API gateways), read `c.req.header('x-correlation-id')` and fall back to `crypto.randomUUID()`. Pass the ID back in the response via `c.header('x-correlation-id', id)` so clients can reference it. Bind your logger instance to the app via `app.use('*', correlationMiddleware)` before any route handlers — order in Hono is strictly top-down. This pattern works identically across Hono's supported runtimes (Bun, Deno, Cloudflare Workers) since it only uses Web Standard APIs.

bytes.topic: Background Jobs — task queues, scheduled jobs, worker process patterns
bytes.title: Hono Background Jobs — Task Queues and Worker Patterns with Cloudflare Queues
bytes.body: Hono doesn't ship a built-in job scheduler, but on Cloudflare Workers it integrates cleanly with Cloudflare Queues for durable background processing. You define a queue producer inside any Hono route handler using `c.env.MY_QUEUE.send()`, then handle consumption in the same Worker export via the `queue()` lifecycle hook — completely separate from the HTTP request lifecycle. For scheduled jobs, pair Hono with a `scheduled()` export triggered by a Cron Trigger. The cron handler runs in the same Worker isolate, so it shares bindings (D1, KV, R2) without any extra auth. For Node.js deployments using `@hono/node-server`, there's no native queue primitive — reach for BullMQ backed by Redis instead, and wire the worker process separately from the Hono app entry point. Crucially, never block Hono route handlers with long-running work; offload via `c.executionCtx.waitUntil()` for fire-and-forget tasks that must survive response termination. This keeps p99 latency tight while guaranteeing side-effect completion.

bytes.topic: Monitoring — health check endpoints, metrics exposure, distributed tracing
bytes.title: Hono — Health Checks, Metrics, and Tracing in Production
bytes.body: Hono's middleware-first design makes observability clean to implement without heavy dependencies. For health checks, register a `/health` route that probes your DB or upstream dependencies and returns structured JSON — Kubernetes liveness and readiness probes can hit it directly. For metrics, use `hono/timing` to capture per-request durations, then expose a `/metrics` route returning Prometheus-formatted text via a simple counter/histogram store you maintain in a module-level Map. For distributed tracing, Hono integrates with OpenTelemetry via `@opentelemetry/sdk-node` — extract the `traceparent` header in a root middleware using `c.req.header('traceparent')`, create a child span with `tracer.startActiveSpan()`, and propagate context downstream. The `c.set()` / `c.get()` context store is the idiomatic way to thread the active span through handler chains without globals. Combine all three under a dedicated `observability.ts` middleware file and mount it before your route groups so every request is instrumented unconditionally.

bytes.topic: Dev Tooling — hot reload, code generation, scaffolding CLI
bytes.title: Hono Dev Tooling — Scaffold Once, Hot-Reload Per Runtime
bytes.body:
`create-hono` is Hono's official scaffolding CLI. Run `npm create hono@latest` and it prompts for a runtime target — `cloudflare-workers`, `bun`, `nodejs`, `deno`, `vercel`, and more. Each template wires up the correct adapter and configures a hot-reload dev command automatically.

The dev experience diverges sharply by target:
- **Cloudflare Workers**: `wrangler dev` — emulates the Workers runtime locally, including KV, D1, and R2 bindings.
- **Bun**: `bun --hot src/index.ts` — module-level hot reload; the `Hono` instance and routes re-evaluate without restarting the process.
- **Node.js**: `tsx watch src/index.ts` — full restart on file change via tsx's watch mode.

Because Hono abstracts the HTTP layer through adapters (`@hono/node-server`, `hono/cloudflare-workers`), your route logic is runtime-agnostic — only the entry point and dev command differ. Prototype on Bun locally, deploy to Cloudflare Workers, zero route changes needed.

For team-wide scaffolding, the official starter templates live in `honojs/starter` on GitHub — fork and extend them to bake in your middleware stack, env validation, and folder conventions from day one.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Scaffold a new Hono project
npm create hono@latest my-api
# Prompts: select a template target
# → cloudflare-workers | bun | nodejs | deno | vercel ...

# Generated dev commands per target:

# cloudflare-workers — emulates Workers + bindings (KV, D1, R2)
wrangler dev

# bun — module-level hot reload, no process restart
bun --hot src/index.ts

# nodejs — full restart via tsx watch
tsx watch src/index.ts

bytes.topic: Framework-specific — Django batteries-included, FastAPI auto-docs, NestJS decorators, Phoenix LiveView
bytes.title: Hono's OpenAPIHono — Auto-Docs, Validation, and Types from One Zod Schema
bytes.body:
Hono ships `@hono/zod-openapi` — a first-party package that turns Zod schemas directly into an OpenAPI 3.0 spec and a live Swagger UI, FastAPI-style but with full TypeScript inference baked in.

The key primitive is `createRoute()`. You declare method, path, request shape (params, query, body), and response schemas in one object. Hono validates incoming requests against those schemas automatically — `c.req.valid('param')` returns the typed, already-validated value. No separate validation middleware needed.

Swap `new Hono()` for `new OpenAPIHono()`, define routes with `app.openapi()`, then mount the spec with `app.doc()` and the Swagger UI via `@hono/swagger-ui`. The spec is generated live from the same Zod schemas enforcing runtime types.

This eliminates the usual triangle problem: docs drift from code, code drifts from validation, validation drifts from types. With `OpenAPIHono`, one Zod schema is authoritative for all three. Unlike NestJS decorators or Django REST Framework's `@api_view`, there's no reflection magic — just plain objects, Zod, and zero ceremony.
